frozen_components: Llama-3.1-8B-Base model (activations only extracted, not modified)
architecture.summary: Llama Scope trains 256 Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) across all 32 layers and 4 sublayer positions (post-MLP residual stream, attention output, MLP output, transcoder) of Llama-3.1-8B-Base, with 32K and 128K features per SAE. Each SAE is a single-hidden-layer autoencoder that maps activations to a sparse latent space via Top-K gating (selecting K most-active features) and reconstructs via a decoder matrix. Modified Top-K variants incorporate decoder column 2-norms into feature ranking, post-training conversion to JumpReLU thresholds, and K-annealing during early training. Information flows: frozen LM activations → encoder (linear + Top-K gating) → sparse latents → decoder (linear) → reconstructed activations.
trained_components: SAE encoder weights and bias (per layer/sublayer/width)
SAE decoder weights and bias (per layer/sublayer/width)

biblio.title: Llama Scope: Extracting Millions of Features from Llama-3.1-8B with Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: TopK SAEs reduce L0 sparsity from around 150 to 50, while maintaining or improving both explained variance and Delta LM loss, compared to vanilla SAEs holding all other hyperparameters constant

claims.dataset: L-Eval (1.3 million tokens, 166 documents truncated to 8192 tokens)
claims.statement: On long-context data (8192 tokens), the average reconstruction loss in the last 1024 tokens is 0.0086, which is a 12% increase compared to the training data (1024-token sequences). L0 sparsity increases from 50 to 55

claims.dataset: Wildchat subset (4K chat histories evaluated on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct)
claims.statement: For instruction-finetuned Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, replacing L15R activations with those reconstructed by L15R-32x-TopK SAEs increases the language modeling loss by 0.090, compared to 0.162 for the base model, all while maintaining the same L0 sparsity
TopK SAEs learn similar features to Vanilla SAEs as measured by cosine similarity of decoder columns; features with high cosine similarity tend to have similar interpretations

Wider SAEs do learn entirely new features, not just frequent compositions of existing ones; for example, a distinct Brexit feature in L15R-32x-TopK activates exclusively on this topic, whereas the most similar feature in L15R-8x SAEs is a more general Historical Movements feature

claims.dataset: 768 features (128 each from L7R-8x, L15R-8x, L23R-8x, both TopK and Vanilla), evaluated with GPT-4o
claims.statement: About 10% of features are not interpretable (monosemanticity score of 1); no significant difference in monosemanticity scores between TopK and Vanilla SAEs

claims.dataset: Wildchat subset on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (32 LXR-8x and 32 LXR-32x TopK SAEs)
claims.statement: SAEs generalize well to instruction-finetuned models with the exception of L31R-8x and L31R-32x (trained on residual stream activations just before the final layernorm), where no significant degradation in Delta LM loss or increase in L0 sparsity is observed across other SAEs

A limitation of the monosemanticity evaluation is that it focuses on top activations, which may not assess each feature's behavior on low-activating samples; lower activating samples tend to be less relevant to interpretation for vanilla SAE features, and TopK/JumpReLU SAEs prioritize features with higher activation levels
